title: Seun Kuti Carries His Father's Torch Without Being Consumed by It
author: Allastair Voss
body:
[spotify:artist/6rKUtTPAvqDFvZQYz28WEY]

There is a specific kind of pressure that children of great artists carry, and it is different from ordinary pressure because it is public and permanent and attached to a name that doesn't belong only to them. Fela Kuti is not just a father. He is a cultural monument, a political symbol, a musical innovator whose influence on African music and on the global understanding of what music can do politically is genuinely without parallel. Being Fela's son and making music is a condition that could easily consume a lesser character. Seun Kuti is not a lesser character.

I've been listening to his recent work, the Egypt 80 band he inherited from his father, the new compositions that sit alongside a continued engagement with the Fela repertoire, and finding it increasingly difficult to talk about only in terms of the inheritance. Seun's own voice is clear enough now, his own concerns specific enough, his own musical intelligence sufficiently developed, that the father conversation, while not irrelevant, no longer dominates. He is his own artist who carries a legacy, rather than a legacy carrier who happens to be an artist.

The afrobeat he makes is politically engaged in the most uncompromising way. This is music that understands that form and content cannot be separated, that the communal nature of the music, the multiple horns, the percussion section, the chorus vocals, is itself a political statement about collective action and collective joy. You cannot listen to this music and think that music is apolitical. It refuses the depoliticisation of form that most contemporary music accepts as a given condition of commercial existence.

## Egypt 80 as Living Organism

The Egypt 80 band is forty musicians, and Fela created them and Seun has led them for most of his adult life, and the experience of that continuous leadership is audible in the music. This is a band that knows each other. The tightness of the rhythm section, the way the horn lines interact, the particular energy of musicians who have been playing together for years and who have developed their own shorthand, these are qualities that can't be manufactured or accelerated. They accrue through time, through the shared experience of performance, through the specific knowledge that comes from having played the same music in many different rooms for many different audiences.

The music swings in the technical sense, the phrase musicians use for the property of rhythm that makes it feel inevitable and joyful simultaneously, rather than merely metronomically correct, and that swing is partly the band's collective knowledge and partly something that Seun has inherited and developed in his own playing. He was nine years old when he started performing with the band. He's been doing this for his entire conscious life.

There is a particular quality to ensemble playing that reaches this level of integration: individual parts lose their separability inside the whole. When Egypt 80 is fully engaged, you hear the sections as a single breathing organism rather than as forty people playing simultaneously. That is a rare thing. Most bands approach it intermittently. Egypt 80 lives there.

## The Political Argument in the Structure

Afrobeat as Fela built it was formally inseparable from its politics. The long-form compositions, sometimes stretching past twenty minutes in live performance, refused the commodity logic of the three-minute single. The rotating ensemble structure, with different musicians taking prominence at different moments, refused the cult-of-personality logic of rock stardom. The Pidgin English lyrics, accessible to ordinary Nigerians across regional language lines, refused the elite cosmopolitanism of artists who address the Western press first.

Seun inherited all of this and he has not discarded any of it. The compositions are long. The ensemble structure remains collectivist. The political targets are named rather than gestured at. Where his father aimed at the corrupt Nigerian state of the 1970s and 1980s, Seun has updated the address to cover the ongoing extraction of African resources by multinational capital, the persistence of neo-colonial governance structures, and the specific failures of post-independence African states to deliver the futures they promised their citizens. The analysis is more contemporary. The form is continuous.

## On Carrying and Extending

I find myself thinking about what the next generation of afrobeat looks like, what Seun's relationship to contemporary African music, the Afrobeats plural that has exploded globally, the different but related political and artistic energy of artists from across the continent, means for where the music goes. He's not naive about this. He's been in dialogue with contemporary artists, been part of conversations about what afrobeat means and who it belongs to and how it can develop without abandoning its political seriousness.

The torch he carries is not becoming heavier. If anything, the more clearly his own voice emerges, the lighter the inheritance seems. He's not crushed by it. He's using it. That's the only good way to deal with a legacy that large, and watching him do it is its own kind of satisfaction.

The music will outlast all the complications of legacy and succession that make writing about it so fraught. In twenty years what will matter is whether the records were good, and the records are good. Seun Kuti made music that deserves to be heard on its own terms. I'm hearing it that way. I think you will too.

The political dimension of afrobeat, which Fela built into the music's DNA, its very structure, the way it requires collective participation and refuses individual stardom even as it produced a star, is something Seun carries forward without rhetoric. He doesn't talk about it as much as he demonstrates it. The band, the collective experience, the music that can only exist as a shared act, these things are the argument. He makes the argument by making the music. That's the only form the argument should take. Seun Kuti, now past forty, is making the most substantive and politically clear work of his career. The inheritance is not a constraint. It is a foundation, and he is building on it.
